docs.title: Quick Start
docs.description: A 7-step quick start for GTFS·X: set up an agency, calendars, routes, stops, timetables, fares, and export a standards-compliant GTFS feed.
headings: Using GTFS·X on mobile and tablets
docs.text: Docs · Foundations · Quick Start Quick Start The fastest path from a blank canvas to a working GTFS feed. Each step takes a minute or two; the whole guide should land you with an exportable feed in well under an hour. On this page Set up your agency Define service calendars Draw your routes Add stops Build timetables Set fares Validate and export Flex (on-demand) service Analysis tools Keyboard shortcuts & tips Using GTFS·X on mobile and tablets 1. Set up your agency Click Agency in the sidebar. Enter your agency name, URL, and timezone. This identifies who operates the transit service. 2. Define service calendars Click Calendars . Create service patterns (e.g., Weekdays, Weekends) by toggling which days of the week service runs. Set the date range, then open a pattern's Exceptions subpanel to add holiday exceptions — the Add US holidays picker drops in the federal holidays in one step. 3. Draw your routes Click Routes , then Create Route . Give it a name, pick a color, and click Draw Route Shape . Click on the map to place points along the route, then double-click to finish. Snap to road (on by default) snaps your line to the road network. Use the direction picker to draw separate outbound/inbound shapes. Edit Shape (in the route's Shapes tab) to drag, add, or delete points on an existing shape; you can also rename each shape there. Simplify reduces vertex count if a shape has too many points. 4. Add stops Select a route and click Add Stops to Route , or go to Stops . Choose a route from the dropdown, then click Place Stops on Map . Snap to Route (default) places stops on the route line. Freehand lets you place stops anywhere. Click each stop in the sidebar to edit its name and properties. 5. Build timetables Click Timetables and select a route. The bottom panel shows

docs.title: Rider mini-site
docs.description: An auto-generated rider-facing landing page for every published GTFS·X feed: system map, today's service, route list, and contact info, with light branding.
docs.text: Docs · Publishing & distribution · Rider mini-site Rider mini-site Planner Every published GTFS·X feed gets an auto-generated rider-facing landing page at feeds.gtfsx.com/<slug>/ . System map, today's service, route list, contact info — the essentials a rider needs to ride. No template work, no separate CMS, no extra invoice. See it live → The auto-generated mini-site for a published demo feed ( Sunny Valley Transit ). The page reads directly from the feed and updates the next time you publish. What it is For most small and rural agencies, "the website" is an afterthought — a static page on the county or operator site that lists a phone number and a PDF of the schedule. Riders deserve better; staff don't have time. The mini-site is the cheapest possible escape from that bind: as long as you maintain the GTFS feed in GTFS·X and click Publish, the rider-facing page stays current automatically. What's on it Agency name and contact. Pulled from agency.txt — name, public URL, phone. If the URL is set, it's linked from the page header. Service status banner. "Today is Monday — Weekday service" / "Today is Sunday — Sunday service" / "Today is Thursday — No service today." Reads from the calendar to tell riders what's running before they look at the map. Feed expiry warning. If feed_info.feed_end_date is within 14 days, the page surfaces a banner telling riders the schedule is about to expire. If it's already past, the banner reports how many days ago. (Surface area for fixing this is the editor's Calendars panel.) System map. An interactive Mapbox map showing every route and stop, color-coded from the GTFS route colors. Riders can pan, zoom, and click a stop to see schedule details. Route list. Each route as a clickable pill with the route's short name, long name, and color. C

docs.title: Rider propensity
docs.description: Nationwide map layer showing transit-propensity population and jobs as dots — useful for spotting under-served demand pockets when planning routes.
headings: What the evidence supports, segment by segment
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Rider propensity Rider propensity Free A nationwide Demand Dots map layer that draws transit-relevant population and the jobs they could be commuting to as dots on the basemap. It answers two separate questions — who is likely to ride and who depends on transit — and keeps them separate on purpose. Use it to spot under-served pockets when sketching a route or sizing a flex zone. Demand Dots overlaid on the demo feed, with Low income picked in Ridership propensity mode: the segment you picked in strong blue, the rest of the propensity group in a muted blue, everyone else in gray, and jobs in orange. The legend's “1 dot ≈ 40 people” is the ratio in force at this zoom. Where to find it Demand Dots is a basemap layer toggle , not a sidebar panel. Open the basemap control on the top-right of the map (the small square icon below the zoom controls) and check the Demand Dots box. Once it's on you get a mode switch between Ridership propensity and Transit need , then a radio to draw either the whole group ( All likely riders , or Everyone with transit need ) or one segment at a time, and below that, checkboxes for Jobs and Everyone else . The layer draws from zoom 8 (roughly a whole state on screen) all the way in, and every control works at every one of those zooms, including picking a single segment while zoomed out. The basemap control with the Demand Dots checkbox. Toggling it doesn't change the underlying basemap style — it adds the dot layer on top. Two groups, two different questions The layer draws two population groups that transit planning routinely conflates, and that the evidence says genuinely diverge. Pick the wrong one and it will point you at the wrong neighborhoods. Ridership propensity answers who is more likely to actually ride . It is

docs.title: Route visibility
docs.description: Hide and show individual routes on the GTFS·X map. The map and every analysis panel — cost, coverage, Title VI, stop analysis — re-scope to the routes you leave visible, so you can read the numbers for a subset of the network.
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Route visibility Route visibility Every route in the Routes panel has a visibility toggle — the colour swatch. Click it to hide a route from the map. Hiding isn't an edit: the route stays in the feed and still exports. What it changes is the scope every analysis panel computes over — turn routes off and the Cost, Coverage, Title VI, and Stop analysis panels re-tabulate for just the routes still shown. What it is Service planning is comparative. You rarely ask "what does this network cost?" in isolation — you ask "what does coverage look like if I drop the two lowest-ridership routes?", or "how does the equity picture change for the rapid corridors only?". Route visibility lets you answer those questions without deleting anything: hide the routes you want to exclude, read the panels, then toggle them back on. Hiding a route controls which routes are shown (and, by implication, which are excluded from analysis). Nothing is deleted — the hidden routes are still in the feed, still exportable, still one click from coming back. When no routes are hidden, every panel computes over the full feed unchanged. Hiding and showing routes Route visibility is controlled from the Routes panel, where each route row has a colour swatch on the left: Open the Routes panel in the left sidebar. Click a route's colour swatch to hide it from the map. The swatch hollows out to show the route is hidden; click it again to bring the route back. Repeat until the map shows exactly the subset of the network you want to analyse. The eye icon at the top of the list marks the swatch column as the map-visibility toggle. Hiding is per-route and lives only in your current view — it isn't saved with the feed and resets to "all routes visible" when you reopen a feed. To explore a servi

docs.title: Routes & shapes
docs.description: Draw and edit transit route alignments in GTFS·X: snap-to-road via Mapbox Map Matching, freehand drawing, per-direction shape variants, route metadata.
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Routes & shapes Routes & shapes Routes are defined before stops in GTFS·X. The alignment is the most consequential design choice in a transit service, so the editor's workflow locks in the path first, then places stops along it. Each route can have multiple shape variants — typically one per direction of travel. The Routes panel. Each route lists its mode, stop count, and trip count; selecting one opens the per-route detail panel with shape and timetable tabs. Creating a route The new-route panel. Fill in the form on the Details tab, then move to the Stops and Trips tabs to wire up the rest of the route. Click Routes in the sidebar, then Create Route . Fill in: Short name ( route_short_name ) — the public-facing identifier riders use. "5", "Rapid", "1A". Keep it short; long short_names break trip planner UIs. Long name ( route_long_name ) — the descriptive name. "Downtown Express", "Cherry Creek Connector". Usually a corridor or pair of endpoints. Color — the route's brand color, used by the map and by trip planner UIs that visualize routes. Pick something distinguishable from the basemap and from other routes. Route type — bus (3), light rail (0), subway (1), rail (2), ferry (4), cable car (5), aerial lift (6), funicular (7). The GTFS extended route types are supported on import / export but not surfaced in the editor's dropdown today. Agency — only relevant for multi-agency feeds; auto-selects the only agency otherwise. Drawing the route Click Draw Route Shape , then click on the map to place vertices along the alignment. Double-click to finish. The drawing tool has two modes: Snap to road (default): each segment uses the Mapbox Map Matching API to follow the road network. Click two intersections and the line follows the actual road geometry, no

docs.title: Service Alerts
docs.description: Author GTFS-Realtime Service Alerts — detours, delays, stop closures — and publish them to a live feed that trip planners consume, without republishing your schedule.
docs.text: Docs · Publishing & distribution · Service Alerts Service Alerts Planner Post GTFS-Realtime Service Alerts — detours, delays, stop closures, reduced service — to a live feed that Google Maps, Apple Maps, and transit apps consume. Alerts are decoupled from your schedule: posting or expiring one takes effect immediately, with no republish. Composing an alert. A rider-facing header and description, GTFS-Realtime Cause / Effect / Severity, an optional info URL, the entities it affects (whole feed, routes, or stops), and one or more active windows. What it is A GTFS feed is your schedule — the service you intend to run. Real operations diverge: a bridge closes, a stop moves for construction, snow cancels a route. GTFS-Realtime Service Alerts are the standard way to tell riders' apps about those divergences, layered on top of the static feed. GTFS·X gives you a place to write them and a live URL to serve them — without standing up a real-time server. This covers the Service Alerts entity of GTFS-Realtime only. Trip Updates (live arrival predictions) and Vehicle Positions require an AVL feed off your buses and are out of scope. When to use it A detour or stop closure that lasts days or weeks but doesn't warrant reissuing the schedule. A planned reduction (holiday service, weather) you want to flag in advance with an active window. An elevator outage or accessibility note at a specific station. Any rider-facing notice you'd otherwise only post on your website — here it reaches the apps riders actually check. How to author an alert Open a feed and click Service Alerts under Operations in the left rail, then + New alert . Write a Header — the short rider-facing summary (e.g. "Route 5 detour around Main St") — and an optional longer Description . Set Cause , Effect , and Severity

docs.title: Service calendars
docs.description: Build calendar.txt and calendar_dates.txt in GTFS·X: day-of-week toggles, date ranges, holiday exceptions, and calendar_dates-only one-off services.
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Service calendars Service calendars Service calendars describe which days your service actually runs. The Calendars panel writes calendar.txt (weekly patterns within a date range) and calendar_dates.txt (holiday exceptions and one-off changes). Most agencies need two or three patterns — Weekday, Saturday, Sunday — plus a list of holidays that get the Sunday schedule. The Calendars panel. Each service pattern has its day-of-week toggles, date range, and a list of exception dates. Weekly patterns Each row in calendar.txt is a service pattern with a service_id, a date range, and seven day-of-week toggles. In the editor, click Add Service , give it a service_id (use a stable name like WKDY , SAT , SUN — these get embedded in trip records and rider URLs), set the start and end dates, and toggle which days the service runs. Typical patterns: Weekday — Mon–Fri checked, Sat/Sun off. Saturday — Sat only. Sunday and major holidays — Sun only. Add the holidays via exception dates (below). Limited holiday service — used by some agencies for Thanksgiving / Christmas Day when even Sunday service doesn't run. None of the seven day toggles checked; the service runs only on dates that are explicitly added via calendar_dates.txt (see "Exception-only services" below). Date ranges should bracket the live period of your schedule. A feed with stale end_date s gets flagged by validators and rejected by some catalogs. Re-publish with refreshed dates when the schedule pickup happens. Holiday exceptions Click into a service pattern and open its Exceptions subpanel. An exception is a single calendar date that overrides the weekly pattern. GTFS distinguishes two kinds, both shown in the exception list with a badge: No Service ( exception_type=2 ) — service does not run on th

docs.title: Service planning
docs.description: Light service planning in GTFS·X: generate a timetable from a headway, fork the feed into variants and compare their cost, block trips onto vehicles, and set running times in a rail beneath the map.
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Service planning Service planning Planner GTFS·X is a feed editor that now does light service planning too: enter a headway and get a timetable, fork the feed into variants and compare what each one costs, chain trips onto vehicles, and set running times. The scheduling tools open in a rail along the bottom of the editor, so the map stays in view while you plan. These tools are built for small and rural agencies. They stay deliberately simple and fully editable: there is no optimizer and no runcutting. The generated timetable and the auto-blocking are starting points you adjust, not black-box answers. The scheduling rail The scheduling tools live in the bottom rail that runs along the bottom of the editor, beside the validation and visualization panels. The map stays in the center the whole time, so you can keep the route on screen while you schedule it. Click the Timetable tab to generate and edit trips, or the Blocks tab to chain those trips onto vehicles; pick a route and service day inside each one. Drag the rail's top edge to give it more height, or use the maximize button in the rail header to expand it to the full editor when you need room (the map collapses; click again to restore the split). Variants are forked and compared from the header, not the rail. Generate a timetable Drawing a route gives you a shape and stops, but no trips. Rather than hand-entering every departure, open the Timetable tab and use the Generate trips… tool in the Trip tools row. Set a window (say 06:00 to 22:00), a headway ("a bus every 30 min"), and an end-to-end run time, and GTFS·X lays out evenly-spaced trips for you. A live count ("Creates 33 trips") updates as you type. Generate trips reads like a sentence, in an inline drawer. The end-to-end run time is pre

docs.title: Service summary
docs.description: Service Summary in GTFS·X: revenue hours, trip counts per route, peak vehicles, span of service. Sanity-check a feed and prep NTD reporting figures.
docs.text: Docs · Validation & I/O · Service summary Service summary The Service Summary tab surfaces the operational shape of your feed as numbers: weekly revenue hours, total trips per route, peak vehicle count, and span of service. Use it to sanity-check a feed before publishing, prep NTD reporting figures, or compare a proposed alternative against the existing service. The Service Summary tab in the footer. Per-route breakdowns appear below the system totals. What it shows Open the Service Summary tab in the footer. The view is organized by route, with a system-wide summary at the top. Weekly revenue hours — sum of trip durations across all trips on each calendar day in a typical week, multiplied by how often each calendar runs. Trips per week — count of distinct trips operating in a typical week, summed across service patterns. Peak vehicles — the maximum number of vehicles required at any moment. Computed via a sweep-line over trip start/end times. Span of service — earliest first-stop departure and latest last-stop arrival across the route's trips. Service days — which days of the week the route runs on, derived from the calendar pattern of every trip. When to use it Sanity-check a freshly built feed. Does the revenue-hour total match what the agency budget says you operate? A 30% divergence usually means the feed has stale trips or a calendar that's wrong. Prep NTD reporting figures. Service hours and miles are part of the NTD F-30 / S-10 forms. The Service Summary gives a defensible figure — verify against the operator's farebox or AVL data before submission. Compare alternatives. Author two feeds (current vs. proposed) as separate projects, open Service Summary on each, and put the per-route numbers side by side in a board memo. Often more legible than a cost figure alon

docs.title: Stations, levels & pathways
docs.description: Model multi-level stations in GTFS·X: define levels (floors), place stops on a level, and connect platforms with pathways (walkways, stairs, elevators) so trip planners can route riders inside a station.
headings: Placing a stop on a level
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Stations, levels & pathways Stations, levels & pathways Most agencies never need this. A single curbside bus stop is one record in stops.txt and that's the end of it. But a multi-platform rail station, a transit center with an underground concourse, or any facility where riders walk between platforms is more than a point on a map — it has floors and internal walking connections. The Stations panel models that: levels.txt for the floors and pathways.txt for the walkways, stairs, and elevators between them, so a trip planner can give a rider turn-by-turn directions inside the station. When to model a station Reach for this only when a rider has to navigate within a facility — a subway station with platforms on two levels, a multimodal hub where the rail platform and the bus bays are a walk apart, an interchange with a fare-gate line between concourse and platform. If your whole system is on-street bus stops, skip it; the overhead isn't worth it and an empty levels.txt / pathways.txt is better than a half-modeled one. The building blocks come from the GTFS station hierarchy, set per stop in the stop editor via Location type : Stop / platform (location_type 0) — where vehicles actually stop. The default. Station (location_type 1) — the parent that groups platforms, entrances, and nodes together. Other stops point at it via Parent station . Entrance / exit (location_type 2), generic node (3), and boarding area (4) — intermediate points a pathway can connect. A pathway connects any two of these nodes. So the usual shape is: one Station , several child platforms and entrances that name it as their parent, and pathways stitching them together across one or more levels. Levels (floors) Open the Stations panel from the left rail. The top section is Levels .

docs.title: Stop analysis
docs.description: Stop-level diagnostics from a GTFS feed: stop-spacing distribution, consolidation candidates, service intensity per stop, and wheelchair-accessibility completeness.
headings: How to use it in GTFS·X
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Stop analysis Stop analysis Planner Four stop-level diagnostics computed straight from the feed: how far apart your stops sit, which neighbouring pairs are candidates for consolidation, how much service each stop actually gets, and whether your board points carry wheelchair-accessibility data. Every table exports to CSV, and the findings highlight on the map as you read them. The Stop Analysis panel. Stop-spacing distribution up top — median, mean, and percentiles, with too-close / in-target / too-far counts — then a per-route breakdown. The other three diagnostics expand below. What it is Stop placement is one of the highest-leverage, least-examined decisions an agency makes. Stops that are too close together slow every trip and inflate operating cost; stops that are too far apart strand riders. Most agencies never look at the distribution because pulling it out of a GTFS feed by hand is tedious. Stop Analysis does it in one panel — open it on any feed and read four diagnostics computed client-side from the schedule, with no setup and nothing uploaded anywhere. It is a planning aid, not a mandate: the thresholds are defaults you can change, and the consolidation candidates are suggestions to review on the map, not stops to delete blindly. The four diagnostics Stop-spacing distribution A histogram of the distance between consecutive stops across the system, with the median, mean, and the 10th / 90th percentiles called out. Segments are colour-coded against APTA / TransitWiki benchmarks — too close (under ~600 ft), in target (~750–1,320 ft), and too far (over ~2,640 ft) — and the panel tallies how many fall in each band. Below the histogram, a per-route table sorts routes tightest-median-first so you can see which lines drive the pattern. Spacing

docs.title: Stops
docs.description: Place and edit GTFS stops with snap-to-route curbside offset, freehand placement for park-and-rides, duplicate detection, and wheelchair-boarding metadata.
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Stops Stops With a route selected, click along the route line to drop stops. By default each stop snaps to the nearest point on the route and renders offset to the right side of the line — mimicking the curbside convention riders actually experience. Place an off-route stop (park-and-ride, transfer center, off-street terminal) by toggling freehand mode. The Stops panel. The badge on the sidebar shows total stop count; the right pane lists every stop with its name and attributes. Snap-to-route placement Open the Routes panel, click a route, and click Add stops . Then click along the route on the map. Each click drops a stop that snaps to the nearest point on the route line and renders offset to the right side (the curbside convention for North American right-of-the-road traffic). The snap means stops always sit on the route geometry — you don't have to worry about whether your click was perfectly on the line. Stops are added in click order; they're given sequential stop_sequence values when the trip is constructed. Reorder them later by dragging the stop list in the sidebar — useful when you realize the route runs in the opposite direction from how you placed the stops. Freehand stop placement Some stops aren't on a route line — park-and-rides served off the main alignment, transfer centers off-street, the end-of-line at a terminal. Toggle Freehand in the stop placement toolbar and click anywhere. The stop drops at that exact lat/lon with no snap. Freehand stops can still be assigned to a route — the editor maintains the route ↔ stop association through route_stops records — they just don't sit on the route's polyline. Trip planners handle this fine; the polyline is for display, the stop locations are what matter for boarding. Duplicate detection I

docs.title: Timetables & trips
docs.description: Build GTFS timetables in GTFS·X: trip grid, auto-interpolation, duplicate-with-offset for repeating service, frequency editor for headway service.
headings: Estimate times from the road network
Duplicating a trip in the other direction
Patterns: picking the shape a trip belongs to
Rebuilding a pattern: trip-less shapes & Remove All Trips
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Timetables & trips Timetables & trips The timetable editor is a grid: each row is a trip, each column is a stop, each cell is an arrival/departure time. Type the first stop's time, auto-interpolate the rest from the route geometry, and use duplicate-with-offset to spawn the repeating service. For headway-only service, the frequency editor writes frequencies.txt instead. The Timetable tab for the Sunny Valley Blue Route. A two-row header — route, service and direction/split controls on top, the Trip tools row below — sits over the trip grid, one row per trip and one column per stop. The trip grid Open the Timetable from the bottom rail, pick a route, and the grid loads. Rows are trips; columns are the stops in stop_sequence order; cells are times in HH:MM:SS format. The editor accepts shorthand input — type 7:30 or 730 and it auto-formats to 07:30:00 . The selector at the top of the grid — a pattern dropdown — switches which trips you're editing. Each pattern has its own trip list and its own stop order — the columns are that shape's stops. The Edit Stops button in the grid toolbar jumps straight to the route's Stops editor, where you set which stops the pattern serves and their order; the Stops tab has the reverse jump back here — an Open timetable editor button. A stop may appear more than once in a pattern — handy for a loop or out-and-back whose first and last stop are the same. Add it again from the "Add existing" picker (set Show stops from → All stops ) and it becomes a second, independent column here; on export it's written as a repeated stop_id at a distinct stop_sequence . Stop states: timed, interpolated, skipped Every cell in the grid is one of three states. The state decides what (if anything) the export writes for that trip at that st

docs.title: Title VI analysis
docs.description: Title VI equity analysis: compare service levels in minority and non-minority block groups using ACS data, with the four-fifths threshold from FTA C 4702.1B.
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Title VI analysis Title VI analysis Planner Compare service levels across minority and non-minority Census block groups using ACS data, with the four-fifths threshold convention from FTA Circular 4702.1B. Use it to identify equity concerns in an existing or proposed service before a board meeting, a hearing, or a Title VI program update. Title VI analysis on the Sunny Valley feed. Regional minority share at top; minority vs non-minority service averages side-by-side; equity ratio at the bottom with the four-fifths threshold banding. What it is Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits federally-funded programs — including most transit agencies — from discriminating on the basis of race, color, or national origin. For transit specifically, the Federal Transit Administration spells out what that means in FTA Circular 4702.1B ("Title VI Requirements and Guidelines for FTA Recipients"). Agencies receiving FTA funds owe FTA a Title VI program update every three years, plus an equity analysis any time they propose a major service change or fare change. The hard part of doing a Title VI equity analysis is the data plumbing: aligning the GTFS feed with Census block groups, deciding which block groups count as "minority," computing service levels in each, and producing a table that supports the eventual finding. The GTFS·X Title VI panel does that plumbing on a feed you already have open, so you can ask "does this service look equitable?" while you're still designing it — not the night before the public hearing. The regulatory context, briefly Two terms from the FTA circular are worth getting right before reading any tool output: Disparate impact (DI). A facially neutral policy that disproportionately affects a protected group (here: minority pop

docs.title: Transfers
docs.description: Model transfers.txt in GTFS·X: recommended / timed (vehicle waits) / minimum time / not possible. Cross-platform rail, hub-bus, mode-change transfers.
docs.text: Docs · Editor panels · Transfers Transfers transfers.txt tells trip planners how to chain trips together at a connection: whether the connection is recommended, whether the next vehicle holds for the arriving one, how much time is needed to make it. Edit it from the Transfers tab inside the Fares panel. Most agencies don't need any transfer rules at all; the few who do are usually modeling a specific connection that defaults to "no transfer possible" without one. The Transfers tab inside the Fares panel. Empty state shown — most agencies leave it empty. When you actually need this By default, trip planners infer transfers from geography — if two stops are within walking distance, they assume riders can transfer between them with the default walking time. The default is usually right. You only need an entry in transfers.txt when the default is wrong or insufficient: Cross-platform rail transfer at a busy station where the spec's default walk-time is too generous. Adding a "minimum_time = 90 seconds" entry tells the planner to use that instead of the default. Timed bus-to-bus connection at a transit hub where the connecting vehicle holds for the inbound one. Without a "timed transfer" entry the planner can't guarantee the connection. Connection that's possible but discouraged (across a parking lot, requires crossing a freeway exit ramp). A "minimum_time = 5 minutes" entry makes the planner prefer alternatives. Connection that looks possible but isn't (the inbound platform doesn't connect to the outbound platform without leaving fare-paid area). A "not possible" entry tells the planner not to suggest it. Same-stop ferry connection where the vessel turns around with a documented load/unload buffer (BC Ferries' 45-minute pattern at terminal stops, for example). Modeled as a

docs.title: Transit access isochrones
docs.description: From an origin pin, see where a rider can reach on your network in 15 / 30 / 45 minutes — schedule-based RAPTOR routing combining walk access, wait, and in-vehicle time — with the population, jobs, and equity populations inside each contour.
headings: How to use it in GTFS·X
docs.text: Docs · Analysis tools · Transit access isochrones Transit access isochrones Planner Drop a pin and see where a rider can travel on your network within a time budget — combining the walk to a stop, the wait for service, the in-vehicle ride, and the walk from the final stop — drawn as 15 / 30 / 45-minute contours, with the population, jobs, and equity populations reachable inside each. Where Demographic coverage tells you who lives near your stops , access isochrones tell you where a rider can go from a stop : the temporal, schedule-aware companion to the buffer-based coverage analysis. It's a Planner feature. An access-isochrone run on the Sunny Valley feed. The blue contours show where a rider can reach from the origin pin within each time budget (darkest = closest), and the sidebar tallies the residents, jobs, and equity populations inside each. What it is The Access Isochrones panel answers the question no buffer-based tool can: from a given point, what can a rider reach on your network in N minutes? It runs a schedule-based transit router over your in-memory feed and combines three legs of a trip into one reachable area: Access — the walk from the origin pin to nearby boardable stops. Transit — riding the network: waiting for the next departure, the in-vehicle time, and any transfers, computed from your stop_times (and frequencies.txt ) for the chosen service day and departure time. Egress — the walk from each reachable stop out to the surrounding streets, drawn as a real routed walkshed. The union of those reachable areas, clipped to each time budget, is the isochrone. Alongside the map contours, the panel tallies the population , jobs , and equity populations (minority, low-income, zero-vehicle households) inside each contour — the same Census plumbing as the cover

docs.title: Validation
docs.description: Continuous GTFS validation in GTFS·X: errors block export, warnings don't, inline jump-to-entity, summary panel. Aligned with MobilityData's validator.
headings: Common warnings worth addressing before publishing
docs.text: Docs · Validation & I/O · Validation Validation Validation runs continuously as you edit. Errors and warnings appear inline next to the entity that owns them; a Validation tab in the footer lists every outstanding issue. Errors block export — they're spec violations that would make the feed unusable. Warnings allow export but get flagged for review. The editor's rule set is aligned with the canonical MobilityData GTFS Validator, so what passes here passes there. The Validation tab in the footer. When everything passes, the tab shows the all-good state; errors and warnings appear here grouped by severity. Errors vs. warnings Errors are spec violations: required fields missing, references that don't resolve, malformed times, duplicate primary keys. Export refuses to produce a ZIP while errors exist — fix them first. Warnings are conditions that are technically valid but probably wrong: a route with no fare assigned, a stop served by no trips, a service pattern with no holiday exceptions configured. Export proceeds but the warnings travel with the feed in the export summary so you can decide whether to address them before publishing. The editor doesn't second-guess this distinction. If the validator says it's a warning, you can ship; if it says it's an error, you can't. The defaults match MobilityData's published rule set, so feeds that pass here pass that validator. Inline messages Most validation messages appear next to the entity that owns them: a red border on a route with no shape, an inline warning on a stop with no name. Click the message to jump to the editor section that owns the entity — the editor opens the right panel, scrolls to the row, and highlights it. For messages that don't belong to a specific entity (feed-level issues — missing feed_info, stale date ra

learn.title: What is GTFS?
learn.text: What is GTFS? GTFS (the General Transit Feed Specification) is the standard, open format that transit agencies use to publish their schedules and route information so that trip planners, mapping apps, and researchers can consume them. If you've ever gotten transit directions in Google Maps, Apple Maps, Transit, Citymapper, or Moovit, those apps were reading GTFS feeds. The short version A GTFS feed is just a ZIP file containing a handful of CSV-style text files. Each file describes one part of a transit system: agencies, routes, stops, the schedule of trips, the calendar of service days, and so on. Together they describe everything an outside app needs to plan a trip on your service. The format was originally developed by Google and Portland's TriMet in 2005 (it was first called "Google Transit Feed Specification") and has since become the de facto open standard, maintained by the MobilityData non-profit and a community of agency and vendor contributors. What's in a GTFS feed? A minimal feed contains these files (all CSV with a .txt extension): File What it describes agency.txt The transit agency operating the service. routes.txt Each named route, such as the "1 Main St", the "Red Line", or the "Crosstown Express". stops.txt Every stop or station, with latitude and longitude. trips.txt Each individual run of a route on a given service day. stop_times.txt The arrival and departure time of each trip at each stop. calendar.txt Which days of the week each service pattern runs. Optional but commonly included: shapes.txt The actual geometry of each route: the polyline drawn on the map. calendar_dates.txt Holiday exceptions and special service dates. fare_attributes.txt & fare_rules.txt How much it costs to ride. frequencies.txt Headway-based service ("every 10 minutes from 6a

learn.title: What is GTFS-Flex?
learn.text: What is GTFS-Flex? GTFS-Flex is an optional extension to the baseline GTFS specification that adds support for demand-responsive transit (microtransit, dial-a-ride, deviated fixed-route, and any other service where vehicles don't follow a strictly fixed route or strictly fixed schedule). It was formally adopted into the GTFS spec in March 2024. It is not required: fixed-route-only agencies don't need it. But without GTFS-Flex, demand-responsive service is invisible to trip planners, even if you're already running it. Rolling Hills Transit's GTFS-Flex service surfaced in the Transit app as part of MnDOT's statewide rural flex program. The Transit app and OpenTripPlanner-based planners can surface demand-responsive service alongside fixed-route options once an agency publishes a GTFS-Flex feed. Source: Transit app blog, “MnDOT + GTFS-Flex: bringing rural riders into the fold” . Why the base spec isn't enough The original GTFS spec assumes a vehicle leaves stop A at 8:00, arrives at stop B at 8:07, and so on: a deterministic sequence of fixed stops at fixed times. That model breaks down for any service where: The vehicle goes wherever riders book it within a defined zone (microtransit, dial-a-ride). Pickups and drop-offs can happen anywhere along a corridor, not just at marked stops. The route follows a fixed path but will deviate on request (deviated fixed-route). Riders need to book in advance (the trip doesn't run unless someone calls). GTFS-Flex extends the spec to cover all of these cases without breaking compatibility with consumers that only understand fixed-route GTFS. What GTFS-Flex adds Service area zones ( locations.geojson ) Instead of (or in addition to) point stops, you define polygon zones using GeoJSON. Riders can request pickup and drop-off anywhere within

learn.title: How to Publish a GTFS Feed (And What Could Break)
learn.text: How to Publish a GTFS Feed (And What Could Break) Publishing a GTFS feed is not the same as creating one. You can build a perfectly valid feed and still have it not appear in Google Maps a month later. The gap is "publishing": the operational steps between "the zip exists on my laptop" and "Apple Maps shows my bus running on time." This guide walks the five things that have to happen, in order, and the four ways each one breaks in production. It's written for a small-agency staffer or consultant publishing for the first time, or troubleshooting a feed that stopped working. What "publishing a GTFS feed" actually means There's no central GTFS registry that you submit to and get a checkmark. Publishing a GTFS feed means making the zip file available at a permanent public URL, then telling each downstream consumer where to find it. Each consumer fetches the file on its own schedule: daily, weekly, or whenever they feel like it. The downstream consumers that matter: Mobility Database: the canonical open registry, maintained by MobilityData. Trip planners, researchers, and AI tools poll it. If your feed isn't in the Mobility Database, you're invisible to most of the ecosystem. Google Maps Transit: requires separate registration through Google's Transit Partners program. Approval can take weeks; once approved, Google fetches your feed on a schedule they set. Apple Maps Transit: requires separate registration with Apple, similar process. Transit App: picks up most feeds from the Mobility Database automatically; some agencies are added through direct outreach. OpenTripPlanner / regional trip planners: most operators of OTP-based regional planners watch the Mobility Database, but the larger ones have direct lists. So "publishing" is really "host the file + register it in the Mobi

capabilities.name: Agency setup
capabilities.purpose: Enter the agency-level fields every feed needs: agency_name, agency_url, agency_timezone, plus agency_id and NTD ID for FTA reporting.
capabilities.whenToUse: First thing on a new feed, or when agency details, timezone, or NTD ID need fixing.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Agency.

capabilities.name: Service calendars
capabilities.purpose: Define service patterns (which days service runs) in calendar.txt and calendar_dates.txt, including holiday exceptions.
capabilities.whenToUse: Setting weekday/weekend/holiday service, or adding a US-holiday exception day.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Calendars. Exceptions live on the Exceptions tab of a calendar (with an 'Add US holidays' picker).

capabilities.name: Fares (v1 and v2 Areas)
capabilities.purpose: Author fare_attributes/fare_rules (Fares v1), and — with Fares v2 enabled — the Areas editor (areas.txt + stop_areas.txt). Transfers are edited here too.
capabilities.whenToUse: Adding fares to a feed, or defining fare areas.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Fares. Fares v2 tabs appear once you enable 'Fares v2' in Settings.
capabilities.limitations: Fares v2 in-app currently ships the Areas editor only; the rest of the v2 model (fare products, leg/transfer rules, rider categories) is not fully editable yet, though imported v2 files are preserved on export.

capabilities.name: Routes & shapes
capabilities.purpose: Create and edit routes (route_type, names, colors), draw route geometry on the map, and manage each route's shapes/patterns.
capabilities.whenToUse: Adding or editing a route, drawing a route line, or setting route type/color.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Routes. Open a route to reach its Details / Stops / Trips / Shapes tabs.

capabilities.name: Stops
capabilities.purpose: Place, name, and edit stops on the map; set wheelchair_boarding and other stop fields; filter the stop list.
capabilities.whenToUse: Adding stops, renaming, fixing accessibility flags, or finding a specific stop.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Stops. Use 'Place Stops on Map' to add; stop names auto-suggest from the nearest intersection.

capabilities.name: Stations, levels & pathways
capabilities.purpose: Model multi-level stations with levels.txt and pathways.txt (walkways, stairs, elevators between platforms).
capabilities.whenToUse: Building a station complex with pathways/levels.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Stations & pathways' in Settings, then Left rail → Stations.
capabilities.limitations: The Stations nav item appears only when the 'Stations & pathways' feature is enabled in Settings (or the feed already contains levels/pathways).

capabilities.name: Frequency-based service
capabilities.purpose: Author headway-based service (e.g. 'a bus every 15 minutes') in frequencies.txt instead of explicit per-trip times.
capabilities.whenToUse: Modeling turn-up-and-go service by headway rather than a fixed timetable.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Frequency-based service' in Settings, then Left rail → Frequencies.
capabilities.limitations: The Frequencies nav item appears only when the 'Frequency-based service' feature is enabled in Settings (or the feed already has frequencies.txt).

capabilities.name: Blocks (vehicle interlining)
capabilities.purpose: Group the trips a single vehicle runs in sequence (block_id) and visualize/validate them on a Gantt, including the Quick Block auto-blocker.
capabilities.whenToUse: Assigning trips to vehicle blocks or checking for block overlaps.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Blocks' in Settings, then Left rail → Blocks.
capabilities.limitations: The Blocks nav item stays hidden until you enable 'Blocks' in Settings, even if the feed already carries block_id.

capabilities.name: Flex Zones (GTFS-Flex) & booking rules
capabilities.purpose: Author demand-response / paratransit service: draw GTFS-Flex zones (locations.geojson) and attach booking_rules.txt.
capabilities.whenToUse: Modeling dial-a-ride, microtransit, deviated-route, or paratransit service.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Flex Zones (on by default; toggle under 'Demand response' in Settings).

capabilities.name: Cost estimation
capabilities.purpose: Estimate operating cost from service levels; system-level summary is free, per-route breakdown is on the Planner suite.
capabilities.whenToUse: Estimating what a service plan costs to operate.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Costs.
capabilities.limitations: System-level cost is free; the per-route breakdown requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey analysis_basic).

capabilities.name: Stop analysis
capabilities.purpose: Analyze stop spacing/balancing, trips-per-day intensity, and accessibility gaps, with map overlays.
capabilities.whenToUse: Checking stop balance, service intensity by stop, or accessibility gaps.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Stop Analysis.
capabilities.limitations: Accessibility gaps hand off to the Validation 'fill wheelchair_boarding' fix rather than editing stops directly.

capabilities.name: Demographic coverage
capabilities.purpose: Measure population and jobs within walking distance of service using census data; per-route breakdown on Planner.
capabilities.whenToUse: Reporting who your service reaches, or comparing coverage across routes.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Coverage.
capabilities.limitations: System-level coverage is free; the per-route breakdown requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey analysis_basic).

capabilities.name: Transit access isochrones
capabilities.purpose: Drop a pin and see where a rider can reach on your network in 15/30/45 minutes (walk + wait + in-vehicle), with population, jobs, and equity populations in each contour.
capabilities.whenToUse: Showing schedule-based travel-time reach from a point.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Access, then drop a pin on the map.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey access_isochrones).

capabilities.name: Title VI equity analysis
capabilities.purpose: Generate FTA-aligned Title VI reports comparing service levels across minority and low-income populations.
capabilities.whenToUse: Preparing a Title VI service-equity analysis.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Title VI.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey analysis_title_vi).

capabilities.name: Service Alerts (GTFS-Realtime)
capabilities.purpose: Author GTFS-Realtime Service Alerts (detours, delays, stop closures) published to a live feed apps consume, without republishing the schedule.
capabilities.whenToUse: Posting a detour, delay, or stop-closure alert on a published feed.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Service Alerts' in Settings (on by default once the feed is published), then Left rail → Service Alerts.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey service_alerts). Only Service Alerts are authored in-app — not TripUpdates or VehiclePositions.

capabilities.name: Feed variants
capabilities.purpose: Fork a feed into named service alternatives, compare cost and service metrics side by side, and export any variant as a standalone GTFS feed.
capabilities.whenToUse: Comparing service scenarios (A vs B) or exporting an alternative.
capabilities.clickPath: Top bar → variants dropdown → 'Manage variants…' (variants have no left-rail item).
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey variants). Opened from the top-bar variants dropdown, not the left rail.

capabilities.name: Feature settings
capabilities.purpose: Turn advanced GTFS features on/off per feed (Stations & pathways, Frequencies, Blocks, Demand response, Service Alerts, Fares v2, Flag-stop service) so the editor stays lean.
capabilities.whenToUse: Revealing an advanced feature's panel/controls, or hiding ones you don't use.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Settings.

capabilities.name: Transfers
capabilities.purpose: Author transfers.txt — transfer rules between routes/stops (timed connections, minimum transfer times).
capabilities.whenToUse: Defining timed connections or minimum transfer times.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Transfers' in Settings, then edit them from the Fares panel (Transfers tab).
capabilities.limitations: Transfers have no standalone nav section — they're a tab inside the Fares panel, revealed by the Transfers feature toggle.

capabilities.name: Timetables & trips editor
capabilities.purpose: Edit trips and stop_times in a spreadsheet-style grid: per-cell times, arrival/departure split, skip/interpolate/timed stop states, opposite-direction companion view.
capabilities.whenToUse: Entering or editing schedule times, adding trips, or reading inbound/outbound side by side.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab (pick a route + service from the toolbar).

capabilities.name: Validation
capabilities.purpose: Continuously validate the feed against the GTFS spec and surface errors/warnings, many with one-click fixes.
capabilities.whenToUse: Diagnosing why a feed is failing validation, or clearing spec errors before export.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Validation tab.

capabilities.name: Service summary
capabilities.purpose: See a rolled-up summary of the feed's service — trips, spans, and service levels — for a sanity check.
capabilities.whenToUse: Getting a quick overview of what service the feed describes.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Service Summary tab.

capabilities.name: Named snapshots
capabilities.purpose: Keep a history of named snapshots of the feed and restore any prior state.
capabilities.whenToUse: Saving a checkpoint before a big edit, or rolling back.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Snapshots tab.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey snapshot_history).

capabilities.name: Hosted publishing
capabilities.purpose: Publish the feed to a stable public URL riders, regulators, and the Mobility Database can rely on, with a rider mini-site and embeds.
capabilities.whenToUse: Turning a feed into a hosted, shareable GTFS URL.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Publish tab.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey managed_publishing).

capabilities.name: Rider mini-site & embed widgets
capabilities.purpose: Generate copy-paste embed snippets (schedules, route maps, stop times) and a rider mini-site for a published feed.
capabilities.whenToUse: Putting schedules/maps on an agency website.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Embed tab (feed must be published).
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey embeds).

capabilities.name: One-click validation fixes
capabilities.purpose: Auto-fix common validation issues: fill blank first/last-stop times, bulk-fill missing wheelchair_boarding, remove orphan/ghost trips, delete unused stops, clear a route_desc that only repeats the name.
capabilities.whenToUse: A validation message shows a 'Fix' button — clearing a class of errors quickly.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Validation tab → 'Fix' on a message (or 'Fix all N of this type').
capabilities.limitations: Only issues with a registered fix show a Fix button; others are manual.

capabilities.name: Generate shapes from stops
capabilities.purpose: Create shapes.txt geometry for a feed that has stops but no shapes, routing through the stops (snapped to roads or straight lines).
capabilities.whenToUse: A feed imports without shapes and you want route lines on the map / in the export.
capabilities.clickPath: Post-import success screen → 'Generate shapes from stops', or Bottom panel → Validation tab → the 'Generate shapes' fix. Both open the Shapes-from-Stops dialog (Snap to roads vs straight lines).
capabilities.limitations: Uses Mapbox Directions through stops as waypoints; quality depends on the stop sequence. Snap-to-roads needs road-network coverage.

capabilities.name: Snap drawn routes to roads
capabilities.purpose: Snap a hand-drawn route line to the road network (Mapbox Map Matching) so shapes follow streets.
capabilities.whenToUse: Drawing a route and wanting it to hug the roads.
capabilities.clickPath: 'Snap to road' is a global setting (on by default); it applies when you finish drawing a route on the map.
capabilities.limitations: If the match looks wrong you can keep the raw drawn line instead.

capabilities.name: Simplify shape
capabilities.purpose: Reduce the number of points in a shape (Ramer–Douglas–Peucker) for a lighter, cleaner geometry, with a live preview.
capabilities.whenToUse: A shape has too many vertices and you want to thin it.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Routes → open a route → Shapes tab → 'Simplify' on a shape (pick a level).

capabilities.name: Generate trips
capabilities.purpose: Generate a set of trips for a route/pattern from either explicit start times or a frequency/headway, laying out stop_times automatically.
capabilities.whenToUse: Building a timetable from scratch for a route.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab → toolbar → 'Generate trips…'.

capabilities.name: Set run time
capabilities.purpose: Re-lay every trip on a pattern to a new end-to-end run time (start times fixed, intermediate stops re-interpolated).
capabilities.whenToUse: Adjusting how long the route takes end to end across all its trips.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab → toolbar → 'Set run time…'.

capabilities.name: Estimate travel times
capabilities.purpose: Estimate stop-to-stop times by driving directions (Mapbox) and lay them into a trip's stop_times.
capabilities.whenToUse: You need realistic times but don't have a timetable to copy.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab → a trip's actions menu → 'Estimate times…'.
capabilities.limitations: Road-driving estimate; review before publishing.

capabilities.name: Interpolate stop times
capabilities.purpose: Fill in intermediate stop_times evenly between the timed stops you've entered.
capabilities.whenToUse: You've set a few key times and want the rest filled in.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab → a trip's actions menu → 'Interpolate stop times'.

capabilities.name: Convert frequencies to individual trips
capabilities.purpose: Materialize a frequency/headway template into real, individual trips (and drop the frequencies row).
capabilities.whenToUse: You authored headway service but need explicit per-trip times.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Timetable tab → a trip's actions menu → 'Convert to trips…' (also offered from the Blocks Gantt).

capabilities.name: Quick Block (auto-blocking)
capabilities.purpose: Automatically group trips into vehicle blocks with a greedy heuristic, then review on the Gantt.
capabilities.whenToUse: You want a first-pass block assignment instead of assigning block_id by hand.
capabilities.clickPath: Left rail → Blocks → the Gantt → '⚡ Quick Block'.
capabilities.limitations: Heuristic first pass; review the result for overlaps.

capabilities.name: Continuous pickup/drop-off (flag-stop service)
capabilities.purpose: Model hail-and-ride / flag-stop boarding between stops via continuous_pickup / continuous_drop_off on routes and stop_times. This is how you build a circulator with continuous pickup and drop-off.
capabilities.whenToUse: On-route boarding/alighting between defined stops (circulators, rural flag-stop service).
capabilities.clickPath: Enable 'Flag-stop service (continuous pickup/drop-off)' in Settings, then set route-level values in the Route editor and per-stop ⚑ overrides in the timetable column header.
capabilities.limitations: Off by default; the controls only appear once the Flag-stop feature is enabled in Settings (or the imported feed already uses continuous_pickup/continuous_drop_off).

capabilities.name: Import & export feeds
capabilities.purpose: Start from an existing feed (upload a .zip, pull from your saved feeds, or import from the Mobility Database) and export a spec-clean GTFS .zip.
capabilities.whenToUse: Bringing a feed in to edit, or getting a finished .zip out.
capabilities.clickPath: Top bar → Import (Upload / My Feeds / Mobility Database) and Export.

capabilities.name: GeoJSON export
capabilities.purpose: Export route shapes and stops as a GeoJSON FeatureCollection for QGIS/ArcGIS/Mapbox and other GIS tools.
capabilities.whenToUse: You need the feed's geometry in a GIS, not as GTFS.
capabilities.clickPath: Top bar → Export → choose GeoJSON.
capabilities.limitations: Exports geometry (shapes + stops), not the full schedule.

capabilities.name: Draft & review links
capabilities.purpose: Share a link to a working feed so stakeholders can review it before you publish.
capabilities.whenToUse: Getting sign-off on a feed before it goes live.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Publish tab → draft/review links.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey draft_links).

capabilities.name: Submit to the Mobility Database
capabilities.purpose: List a published feed in the canonical open transit catalog.
capabilities.whenToUse: Getting the feed discoverable in the Mobility Database.
capabilities.clickPath: Bottom panel → Publish tab → submit to Mobility Database (feed must be published).
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey mobility_db_submit).

capabilities.name: Rider propensity heatmap (demand dots)
capabilities.purpose: Layer ride-likelihood density onto the map to prioritize where to invest service.
capabilities.whenToUse: Deciding where demand is highest.
capabilities.clickPath: Enable the propensity/demand-dots map layer from the map layer controls.
capabilities.limitations: A map overlay, not an editable panel.

capabilities.name: Organizations & team workspaces
capabilities.purpose: Share feeds inside an organization and collaborate with teammates.
capabilities.whenToUse: Working with a team on the same feeds.
capabilities.clickPath: Account → Organizations.
capabilities.limitations: Requires the Planner plan (FeatureKey org_workspace). Feeds sync per account/org but there is no live simultaneous multi-user co-editing.

capabilities.name: Accounts & cloud sync
capabilities.purpose: Sign in to save feeds to your account (R2-backed) and sync across devices; anonymous editing works without an account too.
capabilities.whenToUse: Saving feeds to the cloud or moving between devices.
capabilities.clickPath: Sign in from the top bar; feeds save to your account automatically.

capabilities.name: Deep-link imports
capabilities.purpose: Open GTFS·X on a specific feed via a shareable deep link (e.g. from the Mobility Database).
capabilities.whenToUse: Jumping straight into editing a catalog feed.
capabilities.clickPath: Follow a /import deep link, or the Import dialog's Mobility Database tab.

notSupported.ask: Reroute a route by dragging its existing line on the map
notSupported.reason: Routes are edited by drawing or trimming their shape geometry, not by grabbing an existing route line and dragging it to a new path.
notSupported.workaround: Open the route's Shapes tab and edit/trim the shape, or redraw the line (snap-to-road will follow the streets).

notSupported.ask: Translate stop/route names into other languages (translations.txt)
notSupported.reason: There is no in-app translations editor; GTFS·X doesn't author translations.txt.
notSupported.workaround: Export the feed and add a translations.txt by hand; keep it out of a re-import so it isn't dropped.

notSupported.reason: There are no ferry-specific helpers or on-water route drawing; the map-matching/road tools assume streets.
notSupported.workaround: Create the route, set its route_type to Ferry (4) in the Route editor, and draw the shape by hand (keep the raw drawn line rather than snapping to roads).

notSupported.ask: Generate a live GTFS-Realtime TripUpdates or VehiclePositions feed
notSupported.reason: Only GTFS-Realtime Service Alerts are authored in-app; live TripUpdates and VehiclePositions are not produced by GTFS·X.
notSupported.workaround: Publish TripUpdates/VehiclePositions from your AVL/ITS/real-time system; use GTFS·X for the schedule and Service Alerts.

notSupported.ask: Fully author the whole Fares v2 model in-app (products, leg/transfer rules, rider categories)
notSupported.reason: Fares v2 authoring in-app currently ships the Areas editor (areas.txt + stop_areas.txt); the rest of the v2 model isn't fully editable yet.
notSupported.workaround: Import a feed that already carries those v2 files (they're preserved on export), or hand-edit after export.

notSupported.ask: Real-time collaborative editing with multiple people in the same feed at once
notSupported.reason: Feeds sync to your account/org, but there's no Google-Docs-style simultaneous multi-user co-editing — one editor at a time per feed.
notSupported.workaround: Use an org workspace to share the feed and coordinate who edits when.

notSupported.ask: Find-and-replace or bulk-rename across the whole feed (e.g. rename every stop matching a pattern)
notSupported.reason: There's no global find-and-replace tool; edits are made per entity (stop, route, trip) in their panels.
notSupported.workaround: Export the feed, do the bulk edit in the CSV (stops.txt, routes.txt, …), and re-import.

notSupported.ask: Full edit history / unlimited undo across sessions
notSupported.reason: Undo/redo covers the current editing session only — there's no persistent, unlimited edit history you can scrub back through after a reload.
notSupported.workaround: Use named snapshots (Planner plan) to checkpoint the feed and restore any prior saved state.

notSupported.ask: Export to NeTEx, TransXChange, or another transit-data format
notSupported.reason: GTFS·X exports GTFS (.zip) and GeoJSON only — it doesn't produce NeTEx, TransXChange, or other transit exchange formats.
notSupported.workaround: Export GTFS and convert to the target format with an external converter.

notSupported.ask: Plan a rider trip / journey (door-to-door itinerary) inside GTFS·X
notSupported.reason: GTFS·X is an editor and analysis tool, not a trip planner — it won't compute specific rider itineraries. (Access isochrones show the reachable area from a point, not a turn-by-turn journey.)
notSupported.workaround: Publish the feed and let a trip planner that consumes GTFS (Google Maps, Transit app, OpenTripPlanner) route riders.

notSupported.ask: Import running times from AVL / APC / GPS data to build the schedule automatically
notSupported.reason: GTFS·X doesn't ingest AVL/APC or GPS data; stop times are entered by hand or estimated from driving directions.
notSupported.workaround: Use 'Estimate travel times' (Mapbox driving estimate) as a starting point, then adjust the times in the timetable.

notSupported.ask: Offline maps or a custom basemap
notSupported.reason: The map renders on Mapbox's basemap and needs a connection; there's no offline mode or custom/self-hosted basemap.
